Heterocyclic organic chemistry is a fascinating branch of organic chemistry that deals with the chemistry of cyclic organic compounds with at least one heteroatom in its structure. These heteroatoms, which include nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur, make these structures very versatile in their chemical behavior. In this article, we will explore the subject of heterocyclic chemistry and provide you with tips on how to become knowledgeable in this field.

1. Understand the Basics of Heterocyclic Chemistry

To be knowledgeable with the subject of heterocyclic chemistry, it is essential first to understand the basics of this field. Heterocyclic compounds are cyclic organic compounds that contain at least one heteroatom, which is an atom other than carbon. The heteroatom can be nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, or any other element.

2. Learn About the Different Types of Heterocyclic Compounds

Heterocyclic compounds are classified based on the type of heteroatom present in the ring. The most common heteroatoms found in heterocyclic compounds are nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur. Examples of heterocyclic compounds include pyridine, pyrrole, furan, and thiophene.

3. Understand the Synthesis of Heterocyclic Compounds

The synthesis of heterocyclic compounds is mainly by the process of cyclization of a linear compound that includes a heteroatom in its structure. This process is done through various methods, including the Friedel-Crafts reaction, the Hantzsch pyridine synthesis, and the Knorr synthesis, among others.

4. Study the Properties of Heterocyclic Compounds

Besides electronic and spectroscopic properties of heterocyclic compounds can be very interesting to study and to evaluate based on its applications in many types of industries. The properties of heterocyclic compounds are also dependent on their ring size and the nature of the heteroatom present in the ring.

5. Learn About the Applications of Heterocyclic Compounds

Heterocyclic compounds have a wide range of applications in various industries, including medicine, agriculture, and the chemical industry. Some examples include the use of pyridine in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals, the use of pyrimidine in agrochemicals, and the use of imidazole in corrosion inhibitors.

6. Keep Up-to-Date with Research in the Field

This area of organic chemistry is a relatively new to the chemical science, and there is still an actively ongoing research that is done on these compounds and its derivatives. To be knowledgeable in the field, it is essential to stay up-to-date with current research and trends in heterocyclic chemistry.

In conclusion, heterocyclic organic chemistry is an interesting and diverse field with numerous applications in various industries. To be knowledgeable in this subject, it is crucial to understand the basics of heterocyclic chemistry, learn about the different types of heterocyclic compounds, understand their synthesis and properties, and keep up-to-date with research in the field. With these tips, you can become knowledgeable with the subject of heterocyclic chemistry.

Have you ever encountered the Police Virus on your Windows 7 computer? This virus will lock up your computer and pretend to be an authority demanding payment in exchange for unlocking your computer. Fortunately, this article will show you how to manually remove the virus using another non-infected user who has Administrator access.

Step 1: Start or Switch Using the Non-Infected User's Name

Firstly, you need to log in with the non-infected user's name who has administrator access. If you are already logged in using the infected user's name, switch the user to the non-infected user's name.

Step 2: Start Registry Editor

Open Registry Editor by typing "regedit" in the Start button's search field and selecting "Regedit.exe".

Step 3: Highlight the HKEY_USERS Key and Go to Menu File. Load Hive

In the Registry Editor, highlight the HKEY_USERS key, and then click on the File menu. Select Load Hive.

Step 4: Go to C:\Users\<user>\ and Open the "ntuser.dat" or "ntuser.dat.bhv" File

Navigate to the folder where the infected user's profile is located. Go to C:\Users\<user>\ where <user> is the name of the infected user. Open the "ntuser.dat" or "ntuser.dat.bhv" file. This file is usually a hidden file.

Step 5: You'll Be Asked for a "Key Name". Expand the Hive You Just Loaded

You will now be prompted to provide a "Key Name". Choose any name for the key name, and then expand the Hive you just loaded.

Step 6: Find a Folder Named "Winlogon" in the Left List. On the Right List Find a Registry Key Named "Shell"

After expanding the Hive, find a folder named "Winlogon" in the left list. On the right list, find a registry key named "Shell".

Step 7: Write Down on a Paper the Exact File Name. Right-Click the Word "Shell" and Select Modify.

Take note of the EXACT file name and location shown on the right. Right-click on the word "Shell" and select Modify.

Step 8: Modify This Value to "Explorer.exe" Instead of Anything It Is Already There

Modify the value of the Shell key to "Explorer.exe" instead of anything that is already there.

Step 9: Go to Edit. Find Menu and Type the Virus Info You Wrote Down. Hit Find Next to Find All the Registry Keys Containing the Virus Info.

After modifying the Shell key, go to the Edit menu and select Find. Type the virus info you wrote down earlier and hit Find Next. This will search for all the registry keys containing the virus info.

Step 10: Brutally Shutdown the Computer by Holding Down the Power Off Button on the Keyboard

Brutally shutdown the computer by holding down the power off button on the keyboard.

Step 11: Delete the Virus Files

After turning on the computer again, navigate to the infected user's folder and delete the virus files.

Step 12: Rename or Delete the Files

Rename or delete the files located in the folder as shown earlier on the right.

Step 13: After Everything Is Checked and OK You Should Completely Delete the Virus Files

After everything is checked and verified OK, completely delete the virus files. Congratulations, you have successfully removed the Police Virus using another non-infected user who has Administrator access!

How to Take Care of a Cat

Cats make wonderful pets, but taking care of them requires some effort. From deciding whether to get a kitten or an adult cat, to providing them with proper nutrition, grooming, and medical attention, there are many things to consider. If you want to learn how to take care of a cat, this article will guide you through the process.

Part 1: Choosing Your Cat

Step 1: Decide if you want a kitten or a full-grown cat.

While kittens are undeniably cute, they require a lot of time, attention, and energy. Before deciding to get a kitten, be honest with yourself about whether you can match their energy level. If you're not sure, consider adopting an adult cat that is already litter trained, has less destructive tendencies and is generally more laid back.

Step 2: Consider medical concerns that may come with a specific cat.

Some cats have chronic medical conditions that may require ongoing care and attention. For example, certain breeds may be susceptible to kidney disease or diabetes. Additionally, some cats may have allergies or sensitivities to certain types of food or environmental factors.

Step 3: Consider the amount of time you have for a cat.

Cats require daily attention, including feeding, watering, and playtime. Before getting a cat, make sure you have the time to devote to their needs. On average, cats require about 30 minutes of attention per day, with additional playtime as needed.

Step 4: Calculate whether you can afford a cat.

Cats require food, litter, grooming supplies, and veterinary care, which can add up quickly. Consider whether you have the financial resources to care for your cat over the long term. If you're unsure, consider adopting from an animal shelter where the adoption fee is significantly reduced, the cat is already spayed or neutered, vaccinated, dewormed, and litter trained.

Step 5: Consider adopting a cat from an animal shelter.

There are many cats in need of homes in animal shelters. Adopting a cat from a shelter not only provides a good home for a cat in need, but it also ensures that you are getting a cat that has already been examined and treated by a veterinarian.

Part 2: Caring for Your Cat

Step 1: Encourage the cat to use a litter box.

Most cats prefer the litter box to other parts of the house because of the texture of the litter. Provide a litter box in a quiet, private location and be sure to clean it out regularly.

Step 2: Make the litter box a comfortable place.

Cats prefer a clean and comfortable litter box. Choose a box that is large enough for your cat to comfortably move around in and scoop out the waste daily.

Step 3: Take young or old cats needs into consideration.

Young kittens need extra attention and stimulation, while older cats may require additional support or more frequent vet visits. Adjust accordingly depending on your cat's age and needs.

Step 4: Provide the cat with a scratching post.

Most cats need to scratch to keep their nails healthy and to mark their territory. Provide your cat with a scratching post or pad to prevent them from scratching furniture or other items in your home.

Step 5: Discourage the cat from exploring forbidden surfaces.

Some cats may be more curious than others and may try to explore areas that are forbidden such as kitchen counters, dressers, or shelves. Discourage this behavior by placing sticky tape on surfaces or using a motion-activated deterrent spray.

Step 6: Consider using feline pheromone products.

There are products on the market that use pheromones to calm anxious cats or prevent unwanted behaviors such as clawing or urine marking. Consult with your veterinarian to see if these products may be helpful for your cat.

Step 7: Decide what type of food to feed your cat.

There are many types of cat food available, including wet food, dry food, and raw food. Consult with your veterinarian to see what type of food is best for your cat, as this may depend on their age, weight, and health.

Step 8: Choose a good brand of cat food.

Once you have determined what type of food to feed your cat, choose a high-quality brand. Check the ingredients to ensure that the food is balanced and provides sufficient nutrition for your cat.

Step 9: Follow your cat food's suggested feeding guide.

In general, cats are fed according to age, weight, and activity levels. Be sure to follow the feeding guide on your cat food packaging to ensure that your cat receives the appropriate amount of food.

Step 10: Don't overfeed your cat.

Overfeeding can lead to obesity, which can cause a myriad of health problems for your cat. Be sure to follow the feeding guide and monitor your cat's weight and body condition.

Step 11: Brush your cat depending on their coat needs.

Some cats require regular brushing to keep their coats looking healthy and prevent matting. Consult with your veterinarian or a professional cat groomer to determine whether your cat requires regular brushing.

Step 12: Check the cat for skin conditions as you brush.

As you brush your cat, check their skin for any abnormalities, such as redness, rashes, or irritations. If you notice anything out of the ordinary, consult with your veterinarian.

Step 13: Schedule yearly vet visits.

Just like any member of the family, a cat needs regular medical visits. Schedule yearly check-ups with your veterinarian to ensure that your cat is healthy and up-to-date on their vaccinations.

Step 14: Visit the vet more frequently for kittens.

Kittens require more frequent veterinary visits to ensure that they are growing and developing properly. Consult with your veterinarian to determine how often your kitten needs to be seen.

Step 15: Get your cat spayed or neutered (or "fixed").

Spaying or neutering your cat can prevent unwanted behaviors such as spraying or roaming, and can also help prevent certain cancers and other health issues.

Step 16: Get the cat used to tooth brushing.

To prevent dental problems, get your cat used to having their teeth brushed from a young age. Use a cat-specific toothpaste and brush, and be sure to consult with your veterinarian on proper brushing technique.

Step 17: Schedule professional tooth cleaning if needed.

If your cat's teeth require cleaning, schedule a professional cleaning with your veterinarian. This is typically done under general anesthesia.

Step 18: Make sure your cat gets enough playtime.

Cats need regular playtime to stay healthy and happy. Provide your cat with toys, scratching posts, and interactive play opportunities. 

Taking care of a cat requires time, attention, and effort, but the rewards of having a feline companion can be truly wonderful. Follow these tips, and you'll be well on your way to providing your cat with the best possible care.

In order to qualify for a loan, the lending institution will want to know you are capable of paying the loan back in a timely manner. Having a steady job for at least two years is usually a necessary qualification. If you’ve changed jobs or industries frequently, or otherwise have a checkered employment history, qualifying for a home loan may be more problematic., The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) loans are only available to people who have not had a home foreclosed on within the past three years.Most lending institutions have similar rules.


Any institution that would lend to you despite your having a foreclosure on record within the past three years will likely give you a loan only with an exorbitant interest rate attached to it. Wait several years after any foreclosures in your history to rebuild your credit and obtain a better loan from a more reputable financial institution.

, Your lender should work closely with you to determine your financial needs and provide clear information about their fees and interest rates. You should look into either a broker or a direct lender like a bank or credit union. Usually a broker will be able to get you a better deal. Make sure whatever institution you are dealing with is insured through Veterans Affairs (VA) or the FHA. You could ask your real estate agent to introduce you to a lender, but you should always expand your financing search to include other sources of home loans.

, With your information in hand -- including your employment history, debt, credit history, and other relevant details, your lender will be able to determine whether or not you qualify for a loan.


Be honest when providing your information to the lender. The lender will not verify your information for inaccuracy (the underwriting process will be responsible for this later on in the process), so his or her evaluation of whether you qualify for a home loan is only as good as the info you provide.
Remember, qualifying for a loan is not the same as receiving it. Once you’ve qualified, you’ll need to go through a more formal process in order to gain approval for your loan., Keep in mind, though, that low interest rates (and therefore low monthly payments) mean a longer repayment period, and could mean that you end up paying more over the life of the loan in interest. Always compare loans with similar payback periods, that is, compare 15 year loans only to other 15 year loans and never compare a 15 year loan to a 30 year loan from different lenders.


Checking the Annual Percentage Rate (APR) is the easiest way to compare different loan packages. The APR will tell you the overall price of the loan you qualify for, including mortgage points, lender fees, and interest.
Remember not to confuse APR with the interest rate. APR represents the cost of a loan as a percentage.

, After graduation, you have a six-month grace period during which you don’t need to repay your loans, and your lending institution will only use 1-2% of the balance when calculating your debt ratio (a comparison of your annual debt and income totals).

,


Look for opportunities to make more money. If possible, get a second job, or ask for a raise or promotion at your current job.
If you are married, your spouse’s income will also positively affect your ability to qualify for a home loan.

, The credit reports will help you identify ways to improve your specific score (though these free reports will not give you your actual score). Visit AnnualCreditReport.com to learn more about how to obtain your credit report.


Since each credit agencies have distinct data bases, it is usually good to order reports from each one at the same time, so you can confirm that one does not have an issue that needs to be resolved that the others don't have.
Consult your credit reports before taking out a big loan -- like a home loan -- to get an idea of whether you’ll qualify for a good rate or not.

, Your home loan should be be paid off in monthly payments of around 25% of your monthly after-tax income.For example, if you earn $2,000 each month, your monthly home loan payment should be no more than $500.


Wise lenders usually will not want to extend a loan to someone who cannot pay it back. You are unlikely to qualify for a home loan with monthly payments exceeding 43% of your monthly income, though loans may occasionally close at higher percentages.Though Dodd-Frank legislation has made this rare, some banks will permit or even encourage you to take on an unaffordable loan in the hopes that you default and they can repossess your house or other collateral. Don’t let yourself be roped into a loan that is greater than what you can afford.

, Paying bills late makes potential lenders assume that you are a risk and will stop paying your mortgage altogether.


Rental history is only included in the evaluation of your ability to pay bills if there is not enough credit history available for evaluation. More than one late rental payment in a year could make it hard for you to qualify for a home loan in this case.

, Keep a low credit limit on your card and pay the entire balance off monthly. Also, try to use mostly cash and use your credit card only a few times each month. This way you will not accumulate debt that gets out of control.

, The more credit cards you have, the harder it will be to keep your overall debt low, and the more likely you are to hurt your credit score due to missed or late credit card payments.


If you are early in your credit career, it may be better for your score in the long run to have a few cards, as long as you keep the balances low. Do not try this strategy if you think you will have difficulty keeping track of your balances and making the payments.
Each time you make a request for new lines of credit -- including loans and credit cards -- your credit score drops slightly for one year. Limit yourself to one or two loans each year., If you already have a loan out, pay it off as soon as possible. Paying ahead on your loan will not only reduce the interest you accrue, it will also show lenders you are responsible with your finances.

, However, if you’ve paid off a loan or other debt in a timely manner, you should leave it on the credit report, since it can improve your credit score.


Bad debt (debt that has not been paid within the originally agreed-upon terms) will influence your credit report for seven years. Nothing can be done to remove it, unless it was an error.
Avoid closing old accounts. Keep a paid-off credit card without a balance.

Universal Studios Hollywood is one of the most popular amusement parks in the world, located in Los Angeles, California. There are plenty of things to do and see, including thrilling rides, live shows, and tours of movie studios. To make the most out of your visit, follow these steps:

1. Purchase your Universal Hollywood tickets online or over the phone before your arrival.

To save time and avoid long queues, it's best to purchase your tickets in advance. You can easily buy your tickets online through the official Universal Studios website, or over the phone by calling their customer service hotline. Make sure to print out your tickets or save them on your phone to present them at the entrance.

2. Preview the attractions in the park before your visit.

Check out the official website to see what the park has to offer. This way, you can plan out which attractions you want to prioritize, and which ones you can skip.

3. Pack water, snacks, money and most importantly your park tickets before you leave for the park that day!

It's important to stay hydrated, so pack a water bottle or two for the day. Snacks are a great way to keep your energy levels up, and having cash on hand can save you time when purchasing souvenirs or food. And, don't forget your tickets!

4. Plan to arrive at the park before it opens.

Get to the park early before the crowds start to arrive. This way, you can hit your priority attractions first and avoid long lines.

5. Drive and park at the parking garage located at Universal Studios Orlando.

If you plan to drive, park your car at the designated parking garage at the park. Be prepared for parking fees.

6. Look at the entry area.

Once you arrive at the park, take a moment to get your bearings and study the map to figure out which direction to go first.

7. Enter through the turnstiles.

Present your park tickets at the turnstiles to enter the park.

8. Look at the set up of Universal Studios Hollywood.

As you walk in, take in the sights and enjoy the ambiance of the park.

9. Turn right as you walk in through the gates and follow along.

This will take you to the first set of attractions.

10. Watch the stunt show called WaterWorld.

This live show features water stunts and pyrotechnics.

11. Take on the Shrek 4-D attraction theatre.

This 4-D attraction is an immersive experience featuring your favorite characters from the Shrek franchise.

12. Pass by Universal Plaza to take a vantage point of this central attraction.

This area is home to various shops and restaurants.

13. Watch a showing of how special effects are created for movies at the Special Effects Stage.

This stage show showcases the behind-the-scenes of filmmaking and special effects.

14. Ride the Simpsons Ride, if you love the Simpsons.

This attraction brings the world of the Simpsons to life with exciting ride technology.

15. Take a tour on the Studio Tour.

This guided tour takes you behind-the-scenes of various movie sets and soundstages.

16. Head back out to "center court" and head into the very funny Universal's Animal Actors show.

This show features live animal actors in various comedic acts.

17. Let the kids play in the Super Silly Fun Land area.

This area is perfect for kids with its rides and attractions designed specifically for them.

18. Join up with the minions and Gru in the 3-D ride called "Despicable Me Minion Mayhem".

This ride takes you to the world of Minions and Gru in a 3-D adventure.

19. Find the Lower Lot.

This lower level of the park features additional attractions.

20. Ride the Jurassic Park Ride, which is one of the staple attractions of this park.

This water ride takes you through a prehistoric world of dinosaurs.

21. See the makings of the ways that NBC does things in the NBCUniversal Experience attraction.

This area showcases the history of NBC and its various shows and attractions.

22. Pass by the building Revenge of the Mummy at the very back of the park.

If you're feeling adventurous, this ride takes you through the world of the Mummy with thrilling twists and turns.

23. Look for Transformers The Ride 3D.

This 3-D ride takes you into the world of Transformers with incredible special effects.

Overall, visiting Universal Studios Hollywood is a must-do experience for fans of movies, TV shows, and theme park attractions. With proper planning, you can make the most of your visit and create unforgettable memories.

How to Make Ice Cream with a Machine

Making ice cream at home is a treat that everyone should indulge in from time to time. While there are many ways to make ice cream, using an ice cream machine is one of the easiest and most satisfying methods. With a machine, you can customize your flavors, textures and toppings according to your preferences. Here is a step-by-step guide on how to make ice cream with a machine.

Freeze the Ice Cream Bowl the Night Before

Most ice cream machines require a bowl that has been frozen for at least 12 hours before use. Place the bowl upside down in the freezer to prevent any water droplets from collecting inside. This will ensure that the bowl is completely frozen and ready to use when you're ready to make ice cream.

Set up an Ice Bath

To prevent the ice cream mixture from curdling during the cooking process, set up an ice bath. This involves placing a large bowl filled with ice and water below the cooking pot. The ice bath will cool the mixture down quickly and stop the cooking process once it reaches the desired consistency.

Stir Together the Heavy Cream and the Milk

In a medium saucepan, mix the heavy cream and milk together and heat over medium heat until it reaches a simmer.

Add the Sugar and Whisk It Until It Dissolves

Gradually pour the sugar into the simmering mixture, stirring constantly until it dissolves.

Stir in the Vanilla Extract

Pour the vanilla extract into the mixture and stir well.

Chill the Mixture in the Ice Bath

Place the saucepan containing the ice cream mixture over the ice bath and continue stirring until the mixture cools down completely.

Cover and Refrigerate the Mixture for 3 to 24 Hours

Once cooled, pour the mixture into a container, cover with plastic wrap, and refrigerate for 3 to 24 hours. This step will allow the flavors to meld together and create a rich, creamy texture.

Assemble the Ice Cream Maker

Before adding the mixture to the machine, make sure that it is properly assembled. This includes attaching the paddle or churning blade to the machine's motor and placing the frozen bowl onto the machine.

Pour the Ice Cream Base into Bowl

Take the cooled ice cream mixture out of the refrigerator and pour it into the frozen machine bowl.

Churn the Ice Cream until It Thickens

Turn on the ice cream machine and allow it to churn the mixture until it reaches a thick, creamy consistency. This can take anywhere from 20 to 40 minutes, depending on the type and model of the machine.

Add Any Extras, If Desired

If you want to add any extras like fruit, chocolate chips, or nuts, add them to the machine when the ice cream is almost finished churning.

Transfer the Ice Cream to a Freezer-Safe Container

Once the ice cream is fully churned and has reached a thick consistency, transfer it to a freezer-safe container.

Place a Sheet of Wax Paper Against the Ice Cream

To prevent ice crystals from forming on the surface of the ice cream, place a sheet of wax paper on top of it, pressing down until it sticks to the surface.

Freeze the Ice Cream until It Turns Solid

Place the container with the ice cream into the freezer and freeze it until it turns solid. This will take at least 4 hours, but it’s best to leave it overnight.

Eat the Ice Cream within 2 Weeks

After freezing, the ice cream should be consumed within 2 weeks for best quality and flavor.

In conclusion, making homemade ice cream is a delicious and fun experience that everyone can enjoy. With the right equipment and ingredients, you can create your own custom flavors and enjoy creamy, delicious ice cream any time you want. Follow these simple steps and you'll be on your way to becoming an ice cream master in no time!

They may not tell you that they do not understand, or they may not be following your explanation at all. If they do not understand, they will more than likely not ask the appropriate questions.


If the child is partially verbal or nonverbal, give them time to communicate with alternative communication. This may be typing, sign language, or something else.
If the child cannot use AAC, teaching basic communication should take priority over teaching math.;
, They may have trouble with auditory processing, or translating sounds into words inside their head. If this is the case, written/visual aids can be helpful.


Many concepts can be shown through visual examples, but they are generally paired with verbal instructions. This is where the difficulty lies. Try to use visual cues as often as possible when teaching your child.

, Autistic children may display little interest in subjects outside their special interests. In order to get them to pay attention and learn, you'll have to make it especially interactive and fun.

, Math is often associated with pencils and paper. Fine motor skills are generally affected, and this can create a challenge in terms of mathematics.Learning to write numerals, while manipulating them onto paper can be very overwhelming.


Technology can be utilized to overcome this issue to an extent—the child may find it easier to push buttons and touch screens than to physically grasp a pen.

, It is great to incorporate their interests into math problems. For example, if your daughter loves horses, use toy horses to demonstrate math facts.


If possible, try to find a horse workbook. This may peak interest regarding the work that needs to be completed.

, Although they may seem detached sometimes, autistic children are eager learners. Give them constant reassurance—this is very essential when learning to keep them motivated.


It will also keep them happier and associate learning time with positivity. Instead of dreading it, they may recognize it as a time they get positive attention.

, In terms of language, if your child or student has low verbal skills do not use responses that call for a 'yes' or a 'no'. The confusion associated with the language barrier may interfere with learning math concepts. Using multiple-choice based questions makes this barrier a bit easier to handle.

, Having children replicate your actions is highly successful. For example, you collect four blocks, then they collect four blocks. Show them how you're left with three books, once one is put somewhere else.


In essence, you are training the child to mirror you. She'll slowly see the conclusions of your actions and be able to draw conclusions herself when you're not around.

, Be conscious of their skill level and go from there. Your child may not be at their appropriate grade level, so focus on their skill level instead. Certain areas of math may come easier to them than others. This means that you may need to approach certain math topics at a different skill level than the next.


Just because a child is developmentally behind in terms of speaking does not mean they will be behind in math.
Sometimes, disinterest indicates that the work is not challenging enough. If this happens, try giving them harder work, or leaving a more difficult workbook out and seeing if they interact with it.

, Autistic children struggle with remembering sequences. If the child is able to read, write each instruction down. If the child struggles responding to the first set of instructions, do not confuse them by trying a second set.


Try narrating the steps one at a time as the child completes them. For example, "First, you add two to both sides. Then, you divide both sides by 5. Now you have your answer, x = 7."
Think of them as a foreign language learner. They need a bit more time to process what you're saying, so keep the instructions short and snappy. The easier they are to recall, the better.

, If your child has visual processing difficulties, many children find that black font is easier to process when it is on colored paper (reducing contrast).


A light blue, or tan is a great place to start. These are neutrals that the eye finds easy to process, too.

, Games have always been a light way to learn math as a lot of constructive games have been designed which centers the improvement of math skills of a child. The difficulty level also varies based on the age of the child.


Games are colorful and fun, making them grab children's attention. It is something that today’s kids are very fond of and would do without any resistant. This is like teaching them without even telling that they are being taught.
For instance, famous games like Candy Crush Saga have been working on improving segmentation logic and if we go a little higher on the difficulty level then we have 2048, a game which involves all math skills and facts.

, This makes it a pleasing sensory environment, especially for hypersensitive children. Try to sit near a wall or corner, so sensory input comes from fewer directions.

, The child will feel more comfortable in a familiar place, and will be less likely to be distracted by all the new elements.


For instance, while teaching addition and subtraction, you can take your child to your staircase, give a number to each stair with 0 in the middle, going upward towards +5 and coming downwards towards -5. He is to stand on 0 and asked to add 2 for which he can jump upstairs and if asked to subtract 3, he can come 3 steps down.

, Autistic children learn better if they're in a one-on-one environment.This helps them build self-esteem and confidence. You can focus specifically on their needs. There are also fewer distractions if they're the only ones in the room with you.


It's easier for you, too. Concentrating on one child is difficult enough—to concentrate on more would spread your attention thin.

, Keep the work space clear of various distractions. Visual distractions are very common, and may interfere with the learning process. Only put necessary items on the desk.


Keep all of the child’s work organized and in the same place, too. Keep all resources in one secure location. This way, the child can go back and review their lessons in detail. Lay out each concept clearly, isolating that specific example. That way, each concept is kept separate from other concepts.

, If they are very fidgety, try seating them on an exercise ball, so they can bounce as they work.


To make it fun, try keeping a sensory bin of colorful fidgets, and tell the child to pick one out before the lesson begins.
Their stimming may look unusual to you (e.g. rocking or bouncing). Even if it does, assume that it serves an important purpose. Only intervene if it's unsanitary (e.g. putting objects in their mouth) or harmful (hitting themselves), in which case you can suggest a different stim to them (chewing gum, hitting a pillow).
Excessive fidgeting (i.e., fidgeting to the point that they cannot do any work) indicates that they are stressed, or they are not getting enough exercise.

, If the child cannot do this, they may be unable to tell you when something is wrong, and you will wonder why they aren't focusing like usual. The child should know how to say...


"I need a break, please." (A five-minute break for stimming can calm an overwhelmed child.)
"I'm hungry/thirsty."
"I need to go potty."
"_____ is bothering me."
"I don't understand."
The child also needs to know that you will honor their requests. When they try to advocate their needs, immediately pay attention.

, Math has always been a subject which is learned better if taught with the help of a lot activities. Be it an autistic child or a neurotypical child, activities always help.


One of the most common tools that are used by almost all kids while learning basic addition and subtraction is the abacus. The abacus provides a visual representation that helps them understand the concepts behind basic math.
For instance, a pizza of 8 slices can be used to teach the basics of fractions. A full pizza is 8/8 but if 2 slices are taken out then the fraction becomes 6/8 which means 2 slices are missing. After the class, eat the pizza together. The child will always remember the pizza while doing fractions and put and take imaginary slices out of the imaginary box whenever she is doing a question.

Have you ever wanted to create custom printed mugs, but didn't know how to do it? With dye sublimation, it's easier than you think! In this article, we'll walk you through the steps to create your own custom printed mugs using dye sublimation.

Part 1: Make sure you have what you need.

Before you begin, make sure you have everything you need. You will need:

- Sublimation printer
- Sublimation paper
- Sublimation ink
- Heat press
- Mugs
- Scissors or art knife and ruler
- Heat-resistant tape

Part 2: Have a template.

Once you have your materials, you will need a template for your design. You can create your own template using a software like Photoshop or Illustrator, or you can find a pre-made template online.

Part 3: Once you have downloaded the template.

Once you have your template downloaded, you can begin designing your mug. Be sure to place your design on the template where you want it to appear on the mug.

Part 4: Once you are happy with the position of you logo / logos you are ready to print your artwork.

When you are happy with your design, it's time to print it onto the sublimation paper using your sublimation printer.

Part 5: Now that you have printed your page it should look something like this.

After you have printed your design, take a moment to admire your hard work! Your design should look like what was on your template.

Part 6: Next step is to cut out your designs with your scissors or art knife and ruler.

Now, you will need to use your scissors or art knife and ruler to cut out your designs. Be careful to cut along the edges of your design precisely.

Part 7: Now we are ready to position our print onto our sublimation mug.

Once you've cut out your design, use heat-resistant tape to position it onto your mug. Be sure to center it and make any last-minute adjustments to its positioning before moving on.

Part 8: Now adjust your pressure setting on your mug press so that when you clap your mug in the press it has medium to heavy pressure on it.

With the design in place, it's time to use your mug press to transfer the design onto the mug. Be sure to adjust the pressure setting on your mug press so that it has medium to heavy pressure on it when you clap your mug into it.

Part 9: Now plug in your mug press and set the temperature for 400F / 204C and the timer for 180 seconds and let it preheat to the required temperature.

Set your mug press to the recommended temperature and timer settings, and let it preheat to the required temperature before doing anything else.

Part 10: Once the time is up release the pressure off the press and remove the mug by the handle followed by picking the edge of one the the bits of heat tape on one end of the paper with your fingernail then peeling the paper off the mug in one smooth movement.

Once the time is up, it's time to remove the mug from the press. Release the pressure and remove the mug by the handle. Pick the edge of one of the bits of heat tape on one end of the paper with your fingernail, then peel the paper off the mug in one smooth movement.

Part 11: Now place your mug on a heatproof surface until it has cooled down enough to handle.

Once you've removed the paper, place your mug on a heatproof surface and allow it to cool before handling. And there you have it! Your very own custom printed mug using dye sublimation!

Framing a print is an excellent way to protect and display artwork. A well-framed print can add a touch of elegance to any room, and it also helps preserve the print for years to come. Here's how to frame a print:

1. Measure and cut a mat-board and a mounting board to the overall size calculated to create a mat 4 to 6 inches larger than the print so as to reveal 2 to 3 inches (5 cm to 7 cm) of the mat on all sides of a print.

Before beginning, measure the size of your print and the frame you intend to use. Cut the mat-board and mounting board to the calculated size. The mat-board should be cut to the size of the frame with an additional 4 to 6 inches on each side. The mounting board should be cut to the same size as the print.

2. Obtain a frame matching the dimensions of the finished matted print/mounting board.

Choose a frame that matches the dimensions of your matted print and mounting board. You can find frames at most craft stores or frame shops.

3. Lay the cut-to-size mounting board face up and center the print on it.

Once you have the mounting board cut to size, lay it on a flat surface with the print centered on it. Ensure that the print is straight and centered on the mount.

4. Tape the print to the mounting board with archival tape "hinges" at the top edge only so a print "hangs" and expands and contracts with the humidity.

Use archival tape to create "hinges" on the top edge of the print. This ensures that the print is not permanently stuck to the mounting board and will expand and contract as humidity changes over time.

5. Make a mat for the print using archival mat-board to prevent the print from touching the glass by doing the following.

Cut the archival mat-board to the size that you want your mat. You can choose any color mat that complements your print. The mat will prevent the print from touching the glass which can cause damage over time.

6. Cut the mat-board from the backside along these lines in one stroke for each cut.

Use a mat cutter and cut from the back of the mat-board. Ensure that you cut in one stroke for each cut.

7. Lay the frame face down.

Flip the frame over so that the back is facing up. This will allow you to add the glass, mat, and mounting board to the frame.

8. Clean both sides of the glass to be used in the frame and lay it into the fame.

Before laying the glass in the frame, clean it thoroughly to ensure there are no smudges or fingerprints. Lay the glass into the frame.

9. Lay the display side of finished, cut mat-board into the frame directly on the glass.

Take the finished, cut mat and lay it on the glass. The edges of the mat should overlap the edges of the frame.

10. Lay mounted print in the frame face down on the mat-board installed previously.

Take the mounting board with the print attached and lay it face down over the mat. Place it in the center of the mat.

11. Install backing material of same size as mounting-board/mat into the frame on top of the mounting board just laid in.

Place the backing material on top of the mounting board. It should be the same size as the mounting board and mat.

12. The print is now framed in a professional manner that will last for years.

Secure the backing material into place with the clips attached to the frame. Turn the frame over and admire your newly framed print. It will be protected and look great for years to come.

Brushes are essentially the backbone to the map; it's impossible to have a map without brushes. Brushes are used to create many essential parts of a map including, but not limited to: world geometry, the skybox, area triggers, and displacements.

Brushes are created with the 'Block Tool' located on the panel at the far left of the screen (or alternatively press Shift + B). The block tool can create arches, blocks, cylinders, spheres, spikes, toruses, and wedges, but right now we'll just focus on making a simple block. With the 'Block Tool' enabled (and making sure that at the lower right corner of the screen, the 'Categories' tab is set to 'Primitives' and the 'Objects' tab is set to 'Block') move the mouse cursor to any point on any 2D view grid and drag the left-mouse button to the size of the brush you want. Make sure that it looks right in all three grids (Top, Side, and Front). Press 'Enter' on the keyboard or right-click the mouse in the 2D grid and select "Create Object" to create the brush.;
, Entities are essentially any objects in Hammer Editor that are not world brushes. Most entities perform some sort of action, like lighting a room, teleporting a player to another part of the map, or laying down a route path for an NPC (Non-Player Character).Entities can be split into two categories: brush entities and point entities.

Brush entities are, rather obviously, entities made from brushes. Examples of brush entities include, but are not limited to: trigger brushes, areaportals, hint brushes, and physics brushes.

To make a brush entity, you first need to create a brush (refer to the "Brushes" section above). Once the brush is created, select it with the 'Selection Tool' located at the top of the left panel (or press Shift + S, or simply press the Escape key (Esc) to automatically enable the 'Selection Tool'). With the brush selected, "tie" the brush to an entity by moving the mouse cursor to the 'Tools' menu at the top of the screen and selecting 'Tie to Entity' from the drop-down menu (or, more popularly, by simply hitting Ctrl + T). After you have tied the brush to an entity, the 'Object Properties' menu will pop up on screen. By default, it will become a func_detail entity (beginners probably won't need to know what this is yet). If you want it to become something other than a func_detail brush, simply select one of the many brush entity types from the drop down menu. Hit apply and you now have a brush entity in your map.


Point entities are a little different from brush entities in a few ways. First off, they exist on a single point on the grid by using the "Entity Tool" on the left side of the screen (Shift + E for the keyboard shortcut). Second, they are always accompanied with some sort of visual prop model, even though some entities (such as lights and logic relays) will be invisible in the game. Point entities are used for many things from lighting and ambient sounds, to inserting spawn points, props, and navigation nodes.
To insert a point entity, click the 'Entity Tool' at the panel on the left of the screen (or press Shift + E), then look at the lower right corner of the screen. Under the 'Categories' tab it will say 'Entities' and under the 'Objects' tab will be the name of the currently selected entity. By default, it'll be a spawn point for whatever game you are mapping (ex. "info_player_deathmatch" for Half-Life 2: Deathmatch, "info_player_terrorist" for Counter-Strike: Source, etc.). You can filter through the different point entities by simply typing it in the 'Objects' tab. So if you wanted to insert a light, simply type in "light" in the 'Objects' tab and it'll filter all entities with "light" in it. Once you have the desired entity selected, pick a point on a grid where you'd like to place the entity and click the left mouse button (you may also left click in the 3d camera - keep in mind that if you click on the 3D camera, the entity will be created automatically, while if you click on the 2D grid, you'll have to press enter before it is created). Once it is created and you're satisfied with the placement, press the Escape (Esc) key on the keyboard and you have a point entity in your map.



, Textures define what your brushes look like, as well as assigning material properties to brushes. While most textures are applied on brushes for aesthetic appeal in game, they can be used for optimization and organizational purposes. For example, a trigger_multiple brush entity could receive any texture, but it'd be wise for organizational reasons to texture it with either the tools/toolstrigger or tools/toolsinvisible texture. Another example is tools/toolsnodraw. This is definitely one of the most important textures to use, because any brush face textured with tools/toolsnodraw will not be rendered in the game; therefore, it is wise to create brushes with no draw first, then apply the desired texture to only the brush faces the player can see from the player's point-of-view in the game. Trigger, invisible, and nodraw textures will appear in the 'Auto' visgroup for easy hiding or viewing.

To apply a texture to a brush face, you need to click on the 'Texture Application' tool on the panel to the left of the screen (or simply press Shift + A). Once that is activated, the 'Face Edit Sheet' window will appear, and will be on the 'Material' Tab by default. Around the middle of the window, you'll see what texture is currently selected. You can browse for different textures by clicking the 'Browse' button, but there are literally thousands of textures to choose from. Thankfully, there is a filter for you to use, so if you have an idea of what texture you want, like a wall texture for instance, just type "wall" in the texture filter and all textures with "wall" will be visible in the texture browser. To see all textures again, simply clear the filter box. Once you have found a suitable texture, double-click it and the texture browser will close, taking you back to the Face Edit Sheet. If you already have a brush made, move the mouse cursor over to the 3D window, navigate over to the brush if necessary by pressing "Z" and then using WSAD to move to the brush, and right-click on the brush face(s) that you want to texture. Once you're satisfied, close the Face Edit Sheet.
To apply a texture to the entire brush at once, as opposed to just one face at a time, first select the texture you wish to use. Next, select the brush you wish to edit. Finally, with the brush still selected, move your mouse cursor over to the "Apply Current Texture" tool (no keyboard shortcuts, unfortunately). Select the tool and the entire brush will now be covered in the selected texture.

, Please note that the following map will be made under the Counter-Strike: Source configurations, so there may be discrepancies if you're mapping for another game. If there are any major game-specific steps that are required, this article will address those issues., This will give you the space necessary to create the map.

, It will save you a lot of headaches in the long run.

, Then drag the left mouse button along the 2D top-view grid to create a box (a "brush") to create the floor. You can make it any dimension you choose, but for this example, it'll be 512(Length), 512(Width), and 32(Height). Press the "Enter" key on the keyboard (or right-click the mouse and select "Create Object") to create the brush.

, For this example, the walls are going to be 512(Length), 32(Width), and 256(Height).

, While holding down the "Shift" key (holding "Shift" and dragging clones brushes), drag the newly cloned wall brush to the opposite end of the room.

, Left-click on the cloned brushes once to enter "Rotate" mode (represented by a circle on the corners of the selected brushes) and rotate them 90 degrees and line them flush with the floor and other walls.

, There shouldn't be any gaps in the room.

, Filter with key words such as "wall", "floor", "ceiling", etc. Remember to texture only the faces that the player will be able to see.


Textures that have nothing to do with your theme will look ugly in the final product.

, Regardless of what game you're mapping for, there should always be a player spawn point, so that will be the first entity in this map. Place an info_player_terrorist entity (or whatever respective spawn entity the game utilizes) anywhere in the map with the entity tool. In the case of Counter-Strike: Source, make sure the spawn entities are not touching the floor or they will be stuck and won't be able to enter the map (i.e. "Team is Full" bug).

, For example, if a weapon needs to be reached, make the player look at that weapon. Or, if the player needs to go somewhere, make sure it is facing where it needs to go., This will add light to the map. The entity emits a light that will radiate in all directions, and its brightness and color can be changed.


For example, if you had a BTS map, you could use dim lighting for an eerie vibe. Or, if you had a clean test chamber map, you could put lights in Observation Rooms and/or light strips.

, Having a map with nothing to mess with is just plain boring. Add tons of interactions, for example:

A cube that can go wherever the player puts it
An easter egg control room that has a few buttons that do stuff. That is important.

, Select the tools/toolsinvisible texture, then create a brush big enough to encompass the spawn points of the entire team. Tie the brush (Control+T), then select func_buyzone from the "Class" list under "Class Info". Hit "Apply", then under "Team Number", select which team will be able to use the buyzone (i.e. "Terrorist" buyzone for the Terrorist spawn location).
